title: :1-13 The transfiguration part 1
scripture_text: “Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.” After six days Jesus took with him Peter, James and John the brother of James, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There, he was transfigured before them. His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. Just then there appeared before them Moses and Elijah, talking with Jesus, Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here. If you wish, I will put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.”
devotional:
While he was still speaking, a bright cloud covered them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!’’

When the disciples heard this, they fell face down to the ground, terrified. But Jesus came and touched them. “Get up,”he said. “Don’t be afraid.” When they looked up, they saw no one except Jesus. As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus instructed them, “Don’t tell anyone what you have seen, until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.’’ The disciples asked him, “Why then do the teachers of the law say that Elijah must come first?” Jesus replied, “To be sure, Elijah comes and will restore all things. But I tell you, Elijah has already come and they did not recognize him, but have done to him everything they wished. In the same way the Son of Man is going to suffer at their hands.” Then the disciples understood that he was talking to them about John the Baptist.’’

As Christians, many of us want a faith that is simple and straightforward with no uncertainty, no confusion, no doubt, and few questions. We are the ‘sat-nav’ generation who want to be told how to reach our destination with the least amount of trouble or hardship. And we have adopted the same kind of ’sat-nav’ attitude when it comes to our faith and our relationship with Jesus. We want the quickest and easiest way to spiritual maturity. We want God to tell us every turn we should make, we want our relationship with Jesus to be free from complications, conflict and confusion. Sometimes we want God to micro manage or lives, and levitate from the responsibility of having to make decisions. We want a faith that is neat and sorted, we want a life free from mystery and lostness. But, as we are going to see, sometimes our relationship with Jesus is all about uncertainty.

In Matthew 16 we read the back story of the Transfiguration of Jesus which is recorded in chapter 17:1-13. A number of things happen that help us understand the Transfiguration.

1. Peter confessed that Jesus was the Messiah the Son of the living God. This was one of the high points in the Gospel, Peter was beginning to understand who Jesus was. God the Father was teaching Peter. We will soon see that although Peter had received a revelation from God the Father about his Son Jesus, Peter’s understanding of Jesus was still incomplete.

2. Jesus began to teach the disciples about his suffering and death. Now here was the problem, in the mind of Peter and the other disciples there was no room for a suffering Messiah. Reading Matthew 16:22 you get the impression that Peter was angry with Jesus, The NLT reads, ‘Peter took him aside and corrected him. ‘Heaven forbid, Lord. This will never happen to you.’ The word the NLT translates correct is epitimaō, it has a technical meaning: it is used in Greek religion of gaining control over a spirit or a demon. Robert Guelich (Word Biblical Commentary: Mark.)

3. Jesus had to rebuke Peter by referring to him as a stumbling block, who was under the influence of Satan. This must had been a real shock to Peter; although Peter may have meant well, Jesus could see the work of Satan behind what Peter had said.

4. Jesus begins not only to talk about his cross and his suffering, but he speaks also about their suffering and their cross. We are so familiar with these verses that they do not shock us the way they would have shocked these first disciples. These disciples had seen the Roman crosses on the side of the roads, they had seen the cruel death many had suffered. And now Jesus their Messiah wanted them to carry their cross, a symbol of defeat and shame? One of the songs we sing is ‘This is amazing grace.’ One of the lines is about Jesus bearing our cross, I understand the sentiment behind the words, but Jesus bore our sin so we could bear our cross. We have been designed to live cross shaped lives, it would be a cruel injustice for God to take our ‘cross’ from us.

5. Jesus promised that some of his disciples would see him come in glory in Matthew 16:27-28. There has been a lot of debate about these verses but I think they are referring to Jesus’ Transfiguration. They are also Jesus expressing a pastoral concern, this is how Jesus wanted to deal with the challenges that his disciples were facing regarding their relationship with him.

So as we go with Peter, James and John up to the mount of Transfiguration what can we learn?
prayer: Father help me accept the fact that your will for my life can or may produce some uncertainty.
html:
:1-13 The transfiguration part 1

Study Text: Matthew 16:28-17
“Truly I tell you, some who are standing here will not taste death before they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom.” After six days Jesus took with him Peter, James and John the brother of James, and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There, he was transfigured before them. His face shone like the sun, and his clothes became as white as the light. Just then there appeared before them Moses and Elijah, talking with Jesus, Peter said to Jesus, “Lord, it is good for us to be here. If you wish, I will put up three shelters—one for you, one for Moses and one for Elijah.”

While he was still speaking, a bright cloud covered them, and a voice from the cloud said, “This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased. Listen to him!’’

When the disciples heard this, they fell face down to the ground, terrified. But Jesus came and touched them. “Get up,”he said. “Don’t be afraid.” When they looked up, they saw no one except Jesus. As they were coming down the mountain, Jesus instructed them, “Don’t tell anyone what you have seen, until the Son of Man has been raised from the dead.’’ The disciples asked him, “Why then do the teachers of the law say that Elijah must come first?” Jesus replied, “To be sure, Elijah comes and will restore all things. But I tell you, Elijah has already come and they did not recognize him, but have done to him everything they wished. In the same way the Son of Man is going to suffer at their hands.” Then the disciples understood that he was talking to them about John the Baptist.’’

As Christians, many of us want a faith that is simple and straightforward with no uncertainty, no confusion, no doubt, and few questions. We are the ‘sat-nav’ generation who want to be told how to reach our destination with the least amount of trouble or hardship. And we have adopted the same kind of ’sat-nav’ attitude when it comes to our faith and our relationship with Jesus. We want the quickest and easiest way to spiritual maturity. We want God to tell us every turn we should make, we want our relationship with Jesus to be free from complications, conflict and confusion. Sometimes we want God to micro manage or lives, and levitate from the responsibility of having to make decisions. We want a faith that is neat and sorted, we want a life free from mystery and lostness. But, as we are going to see, sometimes our relationship with Jesus is all about uncertainty.

In Matthew 16 we read the back story of the Transfiguration of Jesus which is recorded in chapter 17:1-13. A number of things happen that help us understand the Transfiguration.

1. Peter confessed that Jesus was the Messiah the Son of the living God. This was one of the high points in the Gospel, Peter was beginning to understand who Jesus was. God the Father was teaching Peter. We will soon see that although Peter had received a revelation from God the Father about his Son Jesus, Peter’s understanding of Jesus was still incomplete.

2. Jesus began to teach the disciples about his suffering and death. Now here was the problem, in the mind of Peter and the other disciples there was no room for a suffering Messiah. Reading Matthew 16:22 you get the impression that Peter was angry with Jesus, The NLT reads, ‘Peter took him aside and corrected him. ‘Heaven forbid, Lord. This will never happen to you.’ The word the NLT translates correct is epitimaō, it has a technical meaning: it is used in Greek religion of gaining control over a spirit or a demon. Robert Guelich (Word Biblical Commentary: Mark.)

3. Jesus had to rebuke Peter by referring to him as a stumbling block, who was under the influence of Satan. This must had been a real shock to Peter; although Peter may have meant well, Jesus could see the work of Satan behind what Peter had said.

4. Jesus begins not only to talk about his cross and his suffering, but he speaks also about their suffering and their cross. We are so familiar with these verses that they do not shock us the way they would have shocked these first disciples. These disciples had seen the Roman crosses on the side of the roads, they had seen the cruel death many had suffered. And now Jesus their Messiah wanted them to carry their cross, a symbol of defeat and shame? One of the songs we sing is ‘This is amazing grace.’ One of the lines is about Jesus bearing our cross, I understand the sentiment behind the words, but Jesus bore our sin so we could bear our cross. We have been designed to live cross shaped lives, it would be a cruel injustice for God to take our ‘cross’ from us.

5. Jesus promised that some of his disciples would see him come in glory in Matthew 16:27-28. There has been a lot of debate about these verses but I think they are referring to Jesus’ Transfiguration. They are also Jesus expressing a pastoral concern, this is how Jesus wanted to deal with the challenges that his disciples were facing regarding their relationship with him.

So as we go with Peter, James and John up to the mount of Transfiguration what can we learn?

Prayer: Father help me accept the fact that your will for my life can or may produce some uncertainty.
Bible in a year reading plan: - 1 Chronicles 4-6
